title: chrootuser: default to GID 0 when given a numeric --user
body:
When we're given a numeric --user value, default to GID 0 if the numeric ID doesn't correspond to a user entry in /etc/passwd that can provide us with the user's primary group ID.  If we're explicitly given a group to run as, don't look up supplemental groups.

Also test various user:group forms.

comments.body:
@rhatdan Didn't we decide to not use GID 0 in this case for security reasons?

@nalind Is there a reason we want numeric UID to be GID 0 in these cases?
Added a hunk to ensure that `GetAdditionalGroupsForUser()` returns wrapped errors, since `lookupAdditionalGroupsForUIDInContainer()` doesn't, and other functions in this package do.
Nalin I think from a security point of view, it would be better to set the GID=UID in this  case, rather then defaulting to 0, even if Docker did it that way.

This is logic that we use for startup in `buildah run` and for handling `--chown` options to `buildah add`, both of which can affect the permissions that are set on the contents of an image.  That breaks tests that we currently have in `buildah`, and it will break conformance tests when we add them there or here.
I'm not strongly against setting this back to 0. If it's necessary for conformance and image building I think it's worth changing back.

I am fine with 95% of this PR, but I believe that 

podman run -ti --user 3267 fedora /bin/sh

Should not end up with a process running as 3267:0 but with a process running sas 3267:3267.  Defaulting to root group is a bad default.

@rhatdan That's a (maybe _the_) key part of this, though.  The alternative forces users to know and remember that they'll get different behavior depending on which build and runtime tools they're using, which is bound to cause us headaches all the way up the stack.
How about we force the user to specify a group if the UID is not in the /etc/passwd?

That would still not be the behavior that images in the wild have come to expect.  Rejecting those images outright would make it easier to spot these cases, but I think that forcing people to modify their already-built images to keep libpod, and projects that use libpod, from rejecting them would be poorly received.

Well I would argue that this is a corner case and not likely to happen often.  I am willing to push more, when I consider this a security issue.  Users are not getting the level of security they expect.

Can you point out one other tool that would default a GID to root, if the UID was only specified.
I would argue that it is not a corner case:  OpenShift's tests, for origin and imagebuilder, exercise this functionality, and the form is used in examples and documentation.
OpenShift built some insecure assumptions into their tests, and actually rely on users running with the root group.  I believe if they want the root group they should specify the root group.

Ok, I believe the best compromize here is to allow this patch to go in, and then add checks to podman to force a user who specifies a UID that does notexist  in the image to return an error suggesting that they must specify a group.  And not default to 0.

I will look into preparing a patch.

LGTM
